name: John Mayer
description: John Mayer's tone is defined by smooth, dynamic overdrive and lush reverb. His pedalboard has evolved over the years but always centers around transparent drive and expressive dynamics. From his PRS Silver Sky through a Dumble or Two-Rock, Mayer's signal chain is all about feel and touch sensitivity.
famousSongs: Slow Dancing in a Burning Room
Waiting on the World to Change
signatureSound: Clean to crunch with transparent overdrive. Smooth, warm lead tones with rich sustain and subtle reverb.

pedals.name: Klon Centaur
pedals.note: The original Klon is rare and expensive — Soul Food is the most popular affordable clone, capturing that transparent mid-push

pedals.name: Ibanez TS10 Tube Screamer
pedals.note: Mayer uses a vintage TS10 — the TS9 is nearly identical in circuit and tone

pedals.name: Strymon Flint
pedals.note: Tremolo + Reverb combo — the RV-6 covers the reverb side beautifully

pedals.name: Way Huge Aqua-Puss
pedals.note: Analog delay for warm slapback — Carbon Copy is a great alternative with similar character

pedals.name: Boss TU-3 Tuner
pedals.note: Standard tuner on Mayer's board — reliable and ubiquitous

pedals.name: Keeley Compressor
pedals.note: Adds sustain and polish to clean tones — the Compressor Plus is the updated version

name: Jimi Hendrix
description: Hendrix pioneered the use of effects in rock music. His setup was simple but revolutionary — a wah, a fuzz, and a Uni-Vibe were all he needed to change music forever. Playing a right-handed Stratocaster flipped upside-down through Marshall stacks, Hendrix created sounds nobody had heard before.
signatureSound: Aggressive fuzz with vocal wah sweeps, swirling Uni-Vibe modulation, and explosive octave effects.

pedals.name: Dallas Arbiter Fuzz Face
pedals.note: The FFM3 Mini captures the classic germanium Fuzz Face tone in a pedalboard-friendly size

pedals.name: Vox Wah
pedals.note: Hendrix made the wah pedal famous — the V847-A is a faithful reissue

pedals.name: Uni-Vibe
pedals.note: The Uni-Vibe is a unique photocell-based modulation — Phase 90 gets you in the neighborhood

pedals.name: Roger Mayer Octavia
pedals.note: Octave-up fuzz effect heard on Purple Haze and other psychedelic moments

name: David Gilmour
description: Gilmour's tone is all about sustain, space, and emotion. His massive pedalboard features multiple delays, a Big Muff for soaring leads, and subtle modulation. Every note breathes. His playing with Pink Floyd created some of the most recognizable guitar sounds in rock history.
signatureSound: Singing lead tones with enormous sustain, lush delay trails, and tasteful modulation. Clean tones shimmer with compression and chorus.

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Big Muff Pi
pedals.note: The Big Muff is essential for Gilmour's singing, sustaining lead tone

pedals.name: Boss CS-2 Compressor
pedals.note: CS-3 is the modern successor to Gilmour's CS-2 — adds sustain and polish

pedals.name: MXR Dyna Comp
pedals.note: Used alongside the Boss compressor for extra squeeze

pedals.name: TC Electronic 2290 Delay
pedals.note: Studio-grade digital delay — Timeline is the modern equivalent

pedals.name: Boss RV-6 Reverb
pedals.note: Lush reverb for those atmospheric Pink Floyd passages

pedals.name: MXR Phase 90
pedals.note: Subtle phaser on clean passages — adds gentle movement

name: The Edge (U2)
description: The Edge built his entire sound around delay. His rhythmic, dotted-eighth-note delays create the shimmering, atmospheric sound that defines U2. His rack system and pedalboard are among the most complex in rock, but the core idea is simple: delay as a compositional tool.
famousSongs: Where the Streets Have No Name
signatureSound: Shimmering, rhythmic delays that create cascading patterns. Clean tone with chiming overtones and atmospheric depth.

pedals.name: Boss DD-8 Digital Delay
pedals.note: The Edge uses multiple delays — DD series is a staple for the basic patterns

pedals.name: Strymon Timeline
pedals.note: For complex delay patterns and preset recall on stage

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix POG2
pedals.note: Organ-like tones heard on songs like 'Mysterious Ways'

pedals.name: Korg SDD-3000 Delay
pedals.note: Legendary rack delay — Flashback 2 has an SDD preset

pedals.name: TC Electronic Corona Chorus
pedals.note: Subtle chorus for stereo width

pedals.name: Boss TU-3 Tuner
pedals.note: Standard tuner — essential for keeping those delay patterns in tune

name: Kurt Cobain
description: Cobain's setup was deliberately simple and raw. A Boss DS-1 or DS-2 for distortion, a Small Clone for the iconic 'Come As You Are' chorus, and that's about it. His approach proved you don't need a massive pedalboard to write songs that change the world.
signatureSound: Raw, aggressive distortion with dynamic clean-to-heavy shifts. Simple, effective use of chorus for haunting clean tones.

pedals.name: Boss DS-1 Distortion
pedals.note: Cobain's go-to distortion — cheap, simple, effective

pedals.name: Boss DS-2 Turbo Distortion
pedals.note: Used in later years — the DS-1 is the closest in our database

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Small Clone
pedals.note: THE chorus sound on 'Come As You Are' — unmistakable

pedals.name: Tech 21 SansAmp
pedals.note: Used for recording — adds amp-like saturation to DI signal

name: John Frusciante
description: Frusciante's tone blends funk, punk, and psychedelia. His board features a mix of vintage and modern effects with an emphasis on modulation and dynamic playing. His work with Red Hot Chili Peppers showcases one of the most expressive and varied guitar tones in rock.
signatureSound: Funky clean tones with expressive wah, thick fuzz for solos, and swirling vintage chorus and modulation.

pedals.name: Boss DS-2 Turbo Distortion
pedals.note: His signature distortion pedal — the DS-1 is the closest match

pedals.name: Ibanez WH10 Wah
pedals.note: Unique vocal wah tone — Cry Baby is the standard, widely available alternative

pedals.name: Boss CE-1 Chorus Ensemble
pedals.note: Vintage stereo chorus — CE-5 is the modern Boss chorus pedal

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Big Muff Pi
pedals.note: Used for heavy, sustaining fuzz tones on songs like 'Dani California'

pedals.name: MXR Micro Amp
pedals.note: Clean boost for pushing the amp — Soul Food can serve as a transparent boost

name: Justin Chancellor
description: Tool's bassist Justin Chancellor uses an extensive array of effects to create the band's dark, hypnotic soundscapes. His playing is as much about texture and atmosphere as it is about groove. His rig produces sounds that blur the line between bass guitar and synthesizer.
signatureSound: Dark, growling bass with heavy distortion, delay-driven textures, and flanger creating an eerie, otherworldly quality.

pedals.name: Boss BF-2 Flanger
pedals.note: Chancellor's signature flanger sound — the CE-5 covers modulation territory

pedals.name: ProCo RAT
pedals.note: Aggressive distortion for that grinding Tool bass tone

pedals.name: Boss DD-3 Delay
pedals.note: Short delay for rhythmic patterns — DD-7 is the modern successor

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Bass Big Muff
pedals.note: Massive fuzz for heavy breakdowns

pedals.name: MXR M-108 10-Band EQ
pedals.note: Shapes his bass tone precisely — same pedal in our database

pedals.name: Dunlop Cry Baby Bass Wah
pedals.note: Wah for those eerie vocal textures on songs like 'Schism'

name: Slash
description: Slash's tone is the sound of hard rock itself. A Les Paul through a cranked Marshall with a wah pedal — that's the foundation. His pedalboard is relatively simple because his tone comes primarily from his fingers, his guitar, and his amp. The pedals are accents, not the main course.
signatureSound: Thick, creamy Marshall overdrive with singing sustain. Expressive wah for leads and a warm, woody clean tone.

pedals.name: Dunlop Cry Baby Wah
pedals.note: Slash's signature wah — his name is literally on a Dunlop signature model

pedals.name: MXR EQ
pedals.note: Shapes his amp tone — the GE-7 is a comparable graphic EQ

pedals.name: Boss DD-3 Delay
pedals.note: Subtle delay for solos — DD-7 is the direct successor

pedals.name: Boss CE-5 Chorus
pedals.note: Chorus for clean sections — notably on 'Paradise City' intro

pedals.name: MXR Dyna Comp
pedals.note: Tightens his clean tone

pedals.name: Dunlop Rotovibe
pedals.note: Uni-Vibe style modulation — Phase 90 captures similar movement

name: Kevin Shields
description: My Bloody Valentine's Kevin Shields created some of the most influential guitar sounds of the 20th century. His use of reverse reverb, heavy tremolo bar abuse, and walls of layered effects defined the entire shoegaze genre. His pedalboard is chaos by design.
signatureSound: Massive walls of reverb-drenched, pitch-bent noise. Reverse reverb, extreme tremolo bar vibrato, and layers of distorted shimmer.

pedals.name: Boss RV-3 Reverb/Delay
pedals.note: Shields' most important pedal — the RV-6 continues the Boss reverb legacy

pedals.name: EHX Small Clone Chorus
pedals.note: Lush chorus adding width to the wall of sound

pedals.name: Boss DS-1 Distortion
pedals.note: Surprisingly simple gain pedal driving those massive textures

pedals.name: Boss TR-2 Tremolo
pedals.note: Tremolo adding rhythmic pulsing to the ambient wash

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Big Muff Pi
pedals.note: Thick, sustaining fuzz for shoegaze walls of sound

name: Tom Morello
description: Morello treats the guitar like a turntable, a synthesizer, and a weapon. His unconventional techniques — toggle switch kills, feedback manipulation, and creative effects routing — made Rage Against the Machine one of the most sonically inventive bands in rock.
signatureSound: DJ-like scratching effects via toggle switch, screaming wah solos, heavy dropped-D riffs with minimal effects but maximum impact.

pedals.name: Dunlop Cry Baby Wah
pedals.note: Essential for Morello's aggressive, screaming lead tones

pedals.name: DigiTech Whammy
pedals.note: Pitch-shifting for those wild, otherworldly sounds — POG2 covers octave territory

pedals.name: Boss DD-3 Delay
pedals.note: Delay for spacey textures and rhythmic patterns

pedals.name: MXR Phase 90
pedals.note: Sweeping phaser on clean sections

pedals.name: Boss EQ
pedals.note: Tone shaping for different guitar/amp combinations

name: Eddie Van Halen
description: Eddie Van Halen revolutionized electric guitar with his tapping technique, divebombs, and the iconic 'brown sound.' His rig was famously modified and experimental — he wired his own pickups, ran a variac on his Marshall, and used minimal effects to get maximum tone.
signatureSound: The 'brown sound' — warm, harmonically rich overdrive from a modified Marshall. Bright, attacking leads with extreme sustain and controlled feedback.

pedals.name: MXR Phase 90
pedals.note: The Phase 90 is all over Van Halen I — 'Ain't Talkin' 'Bout Love', 'Eruption'

pedals.name: MXR Flanger
pedals.note: Jet-like flanging on 'Unchained' — CE-5 covers modulation basics

pedals.name: Boss OC-3 Octave
pedals.note: Octave effects for heavier passages — OC-5 is the latest Boss octave

pedals.name: Dunlop Cry Baby
pedals.note: Wah for specific solo moments

pedals.name: MXR EQ
pedals.note: Tone sculpting for the brown sound

name: Jack White
description: Jack White embraces raw, lo-fi tone with a punk attitude. From The White Stripes through The Raconteurs and solo work, his setup is deliberately rough around the edges. Cheap guitars, heavy effects, and a 'broken is beautiful' philosophy.
signatureSound: Raw, aggressive fuzz and octave effects with a lo-fi, garage-band aesthetic. Heavy distortion meets blues feel.

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Big Muff Pi
pedals.note: Jack White's go-to fuzz for that thick, buzzy distortion

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix POG
pedals.note: Octave effects for the iconic Seven Nation Army bass line (played on guitar)

pedals.name: DigiTech Whammy
pedals.note: Pitch-shifting for wild solo textures

pedals.name: Boss TU-3 Tuner
pedals.note: Even lo-fi players need to tune up

name: Josh Homme
description: Queens of the Stone Age frontman Josh Homme has one of the most distinctive guitar tones in modern rock. Tuned to C-standard with a mid-heavy EQ and tight, gated distortion, his sound is heavy without being traditionally 'metal.'
signatureSound: Tight, mid-heavy distortion with a scooped-then-boosted EQ. Rhythmically precise, almost robotic tightness with an aggressive edge.

pedals.name: Boss GE-7 EQ
pedals.note: Critical to Homme's mid-heavy tone sculpting — exact match in our database

pedals.name: Fulltone OCD
pedals.note: Dynamic overdrive — Morning Glory provides similar transparent drive

pedals.name: Boss TR-2 Tremolo
pedals.note: Tremolo on 'No One Knows' and other QOTSA tracks

pedals.name: Boss DD-7 Delay
pedals.note: Subtle delay for depth — exact match in our database

pedals.name: MXR Phase 90
pedals.note: Occasional phaser for psychedelic passages

name: Kevin Parker (Tame Impala)
description: Kevin Parker single-handedly records Tame Impala's albums, layering guitars with heavy phasing, reverb, and tape-style effects. His sound bridges 60s psychedelia and modern production, creating dense, swirling sonic landscapes.
famousSongs: The Less I Know the Better
Feels Like We Only Go Backwards
signatureSound: Heavy phaser and flanger modulation, drenched in reverb with a vintage, lo-fi warmth. Drums and guitars processed to sound like they're melting.

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Small Stone Phaser
pedals.note: Kevin Parker's go-to phaser — exact match in our database

pedals.name: Boss RV-6 Reverb
pedals.note: Lush reverb for psychedelic washes

pedals.name: Boss DS-1 Distortion
pedals.note: Simple, effective distortion for heavier sections

pedals.name: Boss DD-7 Delay
pedals.note: Tape-style delay repeats that degrade naturally

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Big Muff Pi
pedals.note: Fuzzy sustain for the 'Elephant' riff and similar heavy moments

pedals.name: Boss TR-2 Tremolo
pedals.note: Pulsing tremolo adding movement to clean passages

name: Noel Gallagher
description: Oasis guitarist Noel Gallagher kept things simple: Les Paul, Marshall, and a few key effects. His approach to tone was no-nonsense British rock — big chords, catchy melodies, and enough gain to fill stadiums without overcomplicating things.
signatureSound: Thick British crunch from Les Paul into Marshall, with delay and chorus for stadium-sized atmosphere.

pedals.name: Boss DS-1 Distortion
pedals.note: Gallagher's main distortion — same pedal, same attitude

pedals.name: Boss DD-3 Delay
pedals.note: Delay for those epic, anthemic outros

pedals.name: Boss CE-5 Chorus
pedals.note: Chorus on 'Champagne Supernova' and other atmospheric tracks

pedals.name: Boss TU-2 Tuner
pedals.note: Standard Boss tuner — TU-3 is the current version

name: Dimebag Darrell
description: Pantera's Dimebag Darrell had one of the heaviest, most distinctive guitar tones in metal history. His scooped MXR EQ, the Randall amp's brutal gain, and his aggressive playing style created a sound that was both crushing and melodically expressive.
signatureSound: Extremely scooped mids with crushing high-gain. Razor-sharp harmonics, dramatic wah solos, and that iconic 'buzzing chainsaw' rhythm tone.

pedals.name: MXR M-108 10-Band EQ
pedals.note: THE Dimebag tone secret — scooped mids, boosted highs and lows. Exact match.

pedals.name: Dunlop Cry Baby from Hell
pedals.note: Signature wah for his legendary solos

pedals.name: MXR Flanger
pedals.note: Flanging on specific solos and clean sections

pedals.name: Boss NS-2 Noise Suppressor
pedals.note: Taming the noise from extreme gain settings

name: Billy Corgan
description: Smashing Pumpkins frontman Billy Corgan layered multiple Big Muffs and Op-Amps to create walls of thick, buzzing fuzz that became the band's signature. His approach was more-is-more: stack effects, layer guitars, and bury everything in gain.
signatureSound: Walls of layered fuzz with a buzzing, sustained quality. Multiple distorted guitar tracks creating a massive, enveloping sound.

pedals.name: EHX Op-Amp Big Muff
pedals.note: Corgan's primary fuzz — the Big Muff is the foundation of the Pumpkins' sound

pedals.name: Boss DS-1 Distortion
pedals.note: Additional gain stage for stacking

pedals.name: MXR Phase 90
pedals.note: Phaser for swirling psychedelic textures

pedals.name: EHX Small Clone Chorus
pedals.note: Lush chorus on cleaner passages like '1979'

pedals.name: Boss DD-3 Delay
pedals.note: Delay for ambient buildup sections

name: Kirk Hammett
description: Metallica's lead guitarist Kirk Hammett is famous for two things: his wah pedal and his shredding solos. His wah-driven lead tone is instantly recognizable, and his Dunlop signature wah is one of the best-selling pedals of all time.
signatureSound: High-gain metal rhythm with aggressive, wah-soaked lead tones. Precise, fast alternate picking with melodic phrasing.

pedals.name: Dunlop KH95X Kirk Hammett Wah
pedals.note: His signature wah — the standard Cry Baby is the affordable starting point

pedals.name: Boss DD-3 Delay
pedals.note: Subtle delay on solos for added depth

pedals.name: Boss TU-3 Tuner
pedals.note: Staying in tune with drop-D and standard tuning

name: Andy Summers
description: The Police guitarist Andy Summers created a completely unique guitar sound with sparse, effects-heavy playing. His use of chorus, delay, and compression turned simple chord voicings into shimmering, atmospheric textures that influenced generations of guitarists.
signatureSound: Shimmering clean tones with heavy chorus and compression. Sparse, jazzy chord voicings drenched in modulation and delay.

pedals.name: Roland Jazz Chorus (built-in chorus)
pedals.note: Summers' chorus came from the amp — CE-5 recreates that Boss-style chorus

pedals.name: MXR Dyna Comp
pedals.note: Heavy compression for that squeezed, sustained clean tone

pedals.name: EHX Electric Mistress Flanger
pedals.note: Flanger for the shimmering texture on 'Walking on the Moon'

pedals.name: Boss DD-2 Delay
pedals.note: Rhythmic delay patterns — DD-7 continues the Boss delay tradition

pedals.name: MXR Phase 90
pedals.note: Subtle phasing adding movement to clean chord work

name: St. Vincent (Annie Clark)
description: Annie Clark pushes guitar into unexpected territory. Her playing combines angular, dissonant riffs with warm clean tones and heavy, synth-like distortion. She designed her own Ernie Ball signature guitar specifically to accommodate her effects-driven approach.
signatureSound: Angular, synth-like distortion paired with clean, jazzy chord work. Unpredictable dynamics and creative use of octave and pitch effects.

pedals.name: Boss OC-3 Octave
pedals.note: Synth-like octave bass tones — OC-5 is the latest version

pedals.name: Electro-Harmonix Big Muff Pi
pedals.note: Thick fuzz for her heavier moments

pedals.name: Boss CE-5 Chorus
pedals.note: Chorus for clean shimmer

pedals.name: Boss RV-6 Reverb
pedals.note: Atmospheric reverb for ambient passages

pedals.name: Boss DD-7 Delay
pedals.note: Delay for textural layers — exact match in our database

pedals.name: Boss TU-3 Tuner
pedals.note: Standard tuner
